title: Emergency: Dog Seizure Lasting More Than 2 Minutes - What to Do
meta_desc: A seizure lasting over 2 minutes is a critical emergency for dogs. Learn what to do, why it is urgent, and how to assist your veterinarian.
content_html:
TL;DR: A seizure lasting more than two minutes is a high-priority medical emergency that requires immediate veterinary intervention to prevent brain swelling and organ damage. If safe to do so, recording a video of the event can help your veterinarian provide a faster and more accurate diagnosis.

What is a seizure and what symptoms should I look for in my dog?

A seizure is a sudden, involuntary disturbance in the brain's electrical activity. In dogs, this often manifests as:

  • Muscle twitching or stiffening
  • Loss of consciousness
  • Loss of bladder or bowel control
  • Systemic complications, such as an inability to regulate body temperature, when the event lasts more than two minutes

Is a seizure lasting longer than two minutes considered a medical emergency?

Yes, the urgency level is High. A seizure lasting more than two minutes is a medical emergency because prolonged seizures (status epilepticus) can lead to:

  • A dangerous rise in body temperature
  • Brain swelling
  • Potential permanent organ damage
  • An immediate need to call an emergency veterinarian and transport the pet safely

How does recording a video of the seizure help the veterinarian with triage?

If you can safely record a short video of the seizure, it provides vital information that allows the clinical team to:

  • See the exact nature of the muscle movements and the dog's level of consciousness
  • Confirm the exact duration of the event
  • Differentiate between a primary seizure and other conditions like syncope (fainting) or vestibular disease
  • Reach an accurate diagnosis more quickly upon your arrival at the clinic

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

For a dog experiencing a seizure lasting more than 2 minutes, immediate intervention is required. Diazepam can be administered at 0.5-1 mg/kg/hr as a constant-rate infusion to control persistent status epilepticus. If diazepam is ineffective, propofol can be given as a constant rate infusion at 0.1-0.6 mg/kg/min, potentially followed by a loading dose of phenobarbital at 2-4 mg/kg, IV, every 6 hours for a total of four doses, unless the animal has a pre-existing hepatic condition. In cases where phenobarbital is contraindicated, levetiracetam at 40-60 mg/kg may be administered IV, SC, or rectally. In some instances, inhalation anesthesia may be necessary to control seizure activity, requiring constant monitoring and mechanical ventilation. Phenytoin at 2-5 mg/kg as a slow IV infusion may also be considered.

Chapter: Neurology, Emergency, Pharmacology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1220)

faqs.question: Should I try to hold my dog's tongue during a seizure?
faqs.answer: No. Dogs do not swallow their tongues, and putting your hands near their mouth during a seizure puts you at a high risk of being bitten accidentally.

faqs.question: What is the best way to transport a seizing dog?
faqs.answer: Gently slide them onto a thick blanket to use as a stretcher. Keep their head slightly elevated if possible and ensure they are away from the edges of car seats.

faqs.question: What happens if the seizure doesn't stop?
faqs.answer: Veterinarians will use intravenous medications to stop the seizure activity and may provide cooling measures and oxygen support to prevent further damage.
